paragraphs.body: From Dreams of the Cathedral to Reality
This story explores how places can be different from what we imagine and how comparing two cultures can teach us about diversity and traditions.

annotations.comment: Please meet the minimum word requirement (900-1200 words). For more information, you may view the assignment sheet.

paragraphs.body: Before visiting St. Paul’s Cathedral in Pittsburgh, I imagined it as a tall and ancient building, with golden lights shining through colorful glass windows. I thought it would be a quiet and serious place, like the cathedrals I saw in movies. The name “St. Paul’s Cathedral” made me think of Europe and its old churches, full of history and solemn beauty. I expected to feel a sense of awe and silence as I walked through its grand doors.

annotations.comment: The introduction could be expanded with anecdotes, quotations, or questions, etc.

paragraphs.body: Image Description: A dreamy picture of a grand cathedral surrounded by golden light, with tall spires and colorful stained glass windows.

Conflict: When I finally visited St. Paul’s Cathedral, it was not as I had imagined. The building was beautiful, but the atmosphere felt different. Inside, the people were friendly, and the service was lively. The cathedral was not as quiet and solemn as I had imagined, instead, I realized that my ideas of cathedrals came not from real life but from movies and books. I was surprised by how warm and inviting the place was. Image Description: A realistic picture of a modern cathedral with people walking in and out, showing a warm and inviting place instead of a solemn one.

Rising Action:This made me think of Tengwang Pavilion (滕王阁) in Nanchang, my hometown. Tengwang Pavilion is also famous and grand, but it is full of life. You can hear the sounds of families, see people taking pictures, and even buy snacks from street vendors nearby. It stands tall with its traditional curved roof and red pillars. Compared to the cathedral, the Pavilion is more open and lively, showing Nanchang’s everyday culture instead of being just a historical monument. Both places are important landmarks, but the atmosphere around them is very different.

annotations.comment: To avoid vague pronouns, please clarify what you mean by "this."
Since the narrative is a personal narrative, I would suggest using the first-person perspective consistently throughout the the paper.

paragraphs.body: Image Description: A colorful scene of Tengwang Pavilion, showing traditional Chinese architecture and people enjoying the lively surroundings. Climax:Thinking about both places, I saw how my ideas of Pittsburgh and Nanchang were influenced by stereotypes. I thought all cathedrals were serious and quiet, and I thought all historical places in China were the same. But this comparison showed me how different cultures celebrate history in their own ways. Both places taught me that a place’s real beauty comes from the people and the life around it. I learned that I should experience places with an open mind instead of relying on assumptions.

annotations.comment: You could consider adding more detailed descriptions,  such as specific examples and dialogues, to enrich your comparison.

paragraphs.body: Image Description: A creative mix of cathedral spires and traditional Chinese pavilion roofs, showing the blend of two cultures.

Resolution:This experience helped me understand more about cultural and linguistic diversity. It also taught me not to make assumptions about places before visiting them. Nanchang and Pittsburgh are very different, but they both showed me how important it is to explore new places with an open mind. I now appreciate how each culture expresses its history through architecture and the way people interact with these spaces. Image Description: A symbolic image of a bridge connecting two cultures, with elements of both a modern cathedral and traditional Chinese architecture.
